title: Chest pain with radiation to left arm
content: Chest pain radiating to the left arm, jaw, or shoulder — especially when accompanied by sweating, nausea, or shortness of breath — is a classic presentation of acute myocardial infarction (heart attack). Immediate triage to Cardiology Emergency. Priority 1: Critical. Administer oxygen, obtain ECG within 10 minutes, and activate the cardiac catheterization lab if STEMI is confirmed.

title: Pediatric high fever above 104°F
content: A child with a fever above 104°F (40°C) lasting more than 24 hours requires urgent evaluation. Risk of febrile seizures is elevated. If the child is under 3 months old with any fever above 100.4°F, treat as Priority 1. Assess for signs of meningitis (stiff neck, light sensitivity, rash). Triage to Pediatrics Emergency immediately.

title: Stroke symptoms — FAST protocol
content: FAST: Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call emergency. Any sudden onset of these symptoms indicates a possible stroke. Triage Priority 1 to Neurology Emergency. Thrombolytic therapy window is 4.5 hours from symptom onset. Immediate CT scan required. Do not give food or water — aspiration risk.

title: Severe allergic reaction — anaphylaxis
content: Anaphylaxis presents with hives, swelling of throat/tongue, difficulty breathing, drop in blood pressure, and rapid pulse following exposure to an allergen. Administer epinephrine (EpiPen) immediately. Triage to Emergency Medicine, Priority 1 Critical. Patient may require airway management. Keep patient supine with legs elevated.

title: Difficulty breathing — acute respiratory distress
content: Acute onset shortness of breath with oxygen saturation below 90%, use of accessory muscles, and inability to complete sentences indicates respiratory distress. Could indicate severe asthma, pulmonary embolism, or pneumothorax. Priority 1 — Pulmonology Emergency. Administer oxygen immediately, prepare for possible intubation.

title: Abdominal pain — severe and sudden onset
content: Sudden severe abdominal pain, especially with board-like rigidity, rebound tenderness, or guarding, suggests peritonitis, appendicitis, or ruptured viscus. Priority 2 Urgent — General Surgery Emergency. Obtain CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast. Keep patient nil by mouth. Monitor vitals every 15 minutes.

title: High blood pressure — hypertensive crisis
content: Blood pressure above 180/120 mmHg with symptoms such as headache, visual changes, chest pain, or confusion constitutes a hypertensive emergency. Priority 2 Urgent — Cardiology. Gradual blood pressure reduction required (do not lower too rapidly). Assess for end-organ damage including kidneys, brain, and heart.

title: Diabetic patient — altered consciousness, possible hypoglycemia
content: A known diabetic patient presenting with confusion, sweating, tremors, or unconsciousness may be experiencing severe hypoglycemia (blood sugar below 70 mg/dL). Priority 2 Urgent — Endocrinology Emergency or General ED. Administer oral glucose if conscious, IV dextrose if unconscious. Check blood glucose immediately.

title: Head trauma — fall or blunt force
content: Head injury with loss of consciousness, confusion, vomiting, or unequal pupils may indicate intracranial hemorrhage or traumatic brain injury. Priority 2 Urgent — Neurosurgery Emergency. Immobilize cervical spine. Obtain CT head without contrast urgently. Monitor Glasgow Coma Scale every 30 minutes.

title: Burns — moderate to severe
content: Burns covering more than 10% of body surface area, burns to the face, hands, genitals, feet, or major joints, and all full-thickness burns are Priority 2 Urgent. Direct to the Burns Unit. Immediate fluid resuscitation (Parkland formula). Cover with sterile dressing. Do not apply ice — hypothermia risk.

title: Suspected fracture — limb
content: A patient with limb deformity, inability to bear weight, bruising, or swelling following trauma may have a fracture. Priority 3 Semi-urgent — Orthopaedics. Immobilise the limb with a splint before imaging. Obtain X-ray of suspected area. Assess neurovascular status distal to the injury (pulse, sensation, movement).

title: Urinary tract infection — fever and flank pain
content: UTI with high fever (above 38.5°C), flank pain, and rigors suggests pyelonephritis (kidney infection). Priority 3 Semi-urgent — Urology or General Medicine. Urine culture and blood cultures required. IV antibiotics if systemically unwell. Ensure adequate hydration.

title: Mental health crisis — suicidal ideation
content: A patient expressing active suicidal ideation with a plan or recent attempt requires immediate psychiatric evaluation. Priority 2 Urgent — Psychiatry Emergency. Do not leave patient alone. Remove access to potential means of harm. Involve mental health crisis team. Ensure a calm, non-stimulating environment.

title: Eye injury — chemical splash
content: Chemical splash to the eye requires immediate and continuous irrigation with normal saline or water for at least 20–30 minutes. Priority 2 Urgent — Ophthalmology Emergency. Check and document visual acuity. Alkali burns are more dangerous than acid burns due to deeper tissue penetration. Urgent ophthalmology review.

title: Ear pain and discharge — possible otitis media
content: Mild to moderate ear pain with discharge in an otherwise well patient is consistent with otitis media or external ear infection. Priority 4 Non-urgent — ENT Outpatient. Prescribe analgesia. Antibiotic ear drops if discharge is present. Follow up with GP in 48 hours if not improving.

title: Minor laceration — superficial wound
content: Small superficial cuts with controlled or minimal bleeding and no involvement of tendons, nerves, or deep structures are Priority 4 Non-urgent. Direct to Minor Injuries Unit. Clean with saline, close with steri-strips or sutures if needed. Tetanus prophylaxis if not up to date. Discharge with wound care instructions.

title: Pregnancy — active labour or complications
content: A pregnant patient presenting with regular contractions, rupture of membranes, heavy vaginal bleeding, or sudden severe abdominal pain must be triaged to Obstetrics Emergency immediately. Priority 1 Critical. Assess gestational age, foetal heart rate, and maternal vitals. Prepare delivery suite if labour is imminent.

title: Overdose — suspected drug or medication
content: Suspected overdose with reduced consciousness, abnormal vital signs, or unknown substance ingestion is Priority 1 Critical — Toxicology/Emergency Medicine. Secure airway. Contact Poison Control. Administer naloxone if opioid overdose suspected. Obtain ECG (risk of arrhythmia with many toxins). Do not induce vomiting.

title: Lower back pain — chronic, no red flags
content: Chronic lower back pain without red flags (no fever, no neurological deficit, no trauma, no unexplained weight loss) is non-urgent. Priority 4 — General Outpatient or Physiotherapy referral. Prescribe NSAIDs or paracetamol. Advise continued movement. Refer for physiotherapy. Red flags that would escalate: bowel/bladder dysfunction, bilateral leg weakness.

title: Skin rash — widespread, with fever
content: A widespread rash with fever may indicate a serious systemic infection, drug reaction (Stevens-Johnson Syndrome), or meningococcemia. Priority 2 Urgent — Dermatology or Infectious Disease. Non-blanching petechial rash with fever is a medical emergency (possible meningococcal disease) — escalate to Priority 1. Blood cultures and IV antibiotics must not be delayed.

title: Seizure — first episode or prolonged
content: A first-time seizure or seizure lasting more than 5 minutes (status epilepticus) is Priority 1 Critical — Neurology Emergency. Protect patient from injury. Time the seizure. Administer benzodiazepines if seizure is ongoing. Check blood glucose immediately — hypoglycaemia is a common reversible cause. CT head and EEG required.

title: Vomiting and diarrhoea — dehydration risk
content: Persistent vomiting and diarrhoea with signs of dehydration (dry mucous membranes, reduced skin turgor, tachycardia) is Priority 3 Semi-urgent — General Medicine. IV fluids if oral rehydration is not tolerated. Stool culture if infective cause suspected. Monitor electrolytes. Oral rehydration therapy for mild cases.

title: Dental pain — severe toothache or abscess
content: Severe dental pain with facial swelling, fever, or difficulty swallowing may indicate a dental abscess with risk of spread to deep neck spaces (Ludwig's angina). Priority 3 Semi-urgent — Dental Emergency. If dysphagia or trismus is present, escalate to Priority 2. Prescribe antibiotics and analgesia. Dental drainage may be required.

title: Cardiac arrhythmia — palpitations and dizziness
content: Palpitations with dizziness, near-syncope, or syncope may indicate a significant cardiac arrhythmia. Priority 2 Urgent — Cardiology. Obtain 12-lead ECG immediately. Monitor cardiac rhythm continuously. Common causes include atrial fibrillation, SVT, and ventricular tachycardia. If haemodynamically unstable, escalate to Priority 1.

title: Routine vaccination or health check
content: Patients presenting for scheduled vaccinations, routine blood tests, prescription renewals, or wellness checks are Priority 4 Non-urgent — General Outpatient. No acute medical assessment required. Direct to scheduled appointment queue. Ensure patient has no acute symptoms before proceeding with routine care.
